evidence: Vrijen shared his personal interest in building AI tools as 'brain crutches' for ADHD—filling gaps between therapy, medication, and coaching
Vrijen warned against the 'death by a thousand cuts' of endless unpaid pilots. Instead, seek paid pilots with scoped changes and identify design partners willing to work with the product as-is
He defined product-market fit using the Supreme Court's obscenity standard: 'you know it when you see it'—when you have it, you don't think about it; when you don't, you obsess over it constantly
Chris revealed he's been building his edtech product for almost a year, leveraging AI despite being a former special education teacher rather than a developer

Vrijen shared his own similar path—non-technical background, brief management consulting experience, affinity but not expertise in tech—and described the first year as characterized by face-plants and misplaced trust in technical partners
Chris: 'I took an idea and I did something with it as opposed to just letting it live in my head'
Vrijen emphasized that founder-led sales provides customer research, network building, and messaging refinement—critical activities for early-stage companies
Vrijen strongly advised against seeking VC funding, describing investors unfavorably and noting the difficulty non-technical founders face in being taken seriously
Strong reaction when V warned about investors: 'I think they're not, they're not worth your time, my friend.' He doesn't want VC money.
Vrijen invited Chris to join the Future of Higher Education group (6,000 members globally, ~1,000 in NYC, ~1,000 in Boston), where Vrijen serves as NYC co-lead
The core concept is a 'marketplace of all possible futures' - allowing users to 'jump into' career simulations mapped to real jobs and companies
Max used the metaphor of a 'neopet' - anytime you train or deploy your AI requires 'energy,' which is where they monetize through usage-based pricing
He explained that jobs are essentially 'getting paid to learn,' but gatekeeping makes experience inaccessible
He criticized tools like HackerRank for not grounding results in actual business value - passing an algorithm assessment doesn't prove someone can add value to the company

Engineers more than one step removed from the product were most vulnerable. Those accepting tickets from queues without touching product understanding were let go, while those with ownership of product parts remained
Maximilian argued these roles are 'done' in the current AI-enabled landscape, with many PM positions facing similar elimination unless they manage P&L and have product experience
The consensus is to aim for a sale mid-year but spend the next few months generating revenue first, which Vrijen believes will massively improve their valuation
Maximilian (future simulation product founder) launching new product in approximately one month
He introduced context engineering as the foundational practice: not prompting the AI directly, but cultivating the context it uses to generate desired outputs
He outlined three strategic levels of context engineering: Conversation (tactical), Environment (persistent context), Pipeline (external data integration)
Vrijen argued the core issue isn't a technology or tactics problem but a mental model problem—users collect techniques without understanding how they integrate into effective systems
Threshold rubrics: Ask the AI to create an evaluation rubric, fill it out, assign scores, and iterate output until achieving the highest level
Offensive and defensive prompting: Expansive generation (expand option set) vs. critical tightening (attack, break down, shore up)
Logan demonstrated deep analysis of 18 years of LinkedIn data using Claude Code, having exported 48 CSV files containing 5,000+ messages, connections, search history, and activity data
Network mismatch: Only 3,300 LinkedIn connections versus 45,000 imported contacts from a 2015 data import, revealing two largely disjoint professional networks
Social capital proxy: Inbound versus outbound connection ratio as a measure of professional standing
Posting correlation: Documented 1,800% increase in inbound connections when increasing posting frequency, strong evidence for visibility strategies
Logan shared his metacognitive patterns: consistently ask 'what else?' and 'delight me,' embrace the 'unhobbling' concept from the Anthropic/Claude team
Ben: 'you can unhobble your AI a lot when you give it access to your files and data... when you give the AI its own home'

source_display: Plan Files as Future of Coding
evidence: He emphasized the importance of intentional prompting—documenting AI conversations in plan files that mix prose and code in stepwise fashion. He positioned this as the future of coding
Ben highlighted Zo's integration capabilities: text/email workflows enabling parallel work during passive moments (airport lines, cab rides), using Claude Code via text message to fix code branches
Ben Guo (co-founder of Zo Computer and Vrijen as a power Zo user)
Vrijen Attawar... and Vrijen as a power Zo user
Logan Currie (Careerspan co-founder and AI productivity nerd)
